Q1: I tried to carve out blocks of time throughout the week, usually around 28 hours total, but it wasn't always neat and tidy. Being 26 and juggling other responsibilities, I had to be strategic about when I could really focus. I'm a visual learner, so I'd often map out my week on a whiteboard, color-coding what needed attention. Some weeks flowed smoothly, others felt like I was constantly reshuffling things.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on what was due and what else was going on in my life. I tried to maintain some consistency, like studying in the mornings when my mind was fresh, but deadlines would throw everything off. When assignments piled up, I'd shift into more intensive study sessions. I've learned to be flexible because rigid schedules just don't work for me at this stage of life.
Q3: Usually it was checking if new materials were posted or seeing if there were announcements about upcoming deadlines. Sometimes I'd log in just to watch lecture videos since that's how I learn best. I also liked to see if anyone had posted in the discussions, even though I didn't contribute as often as I probably should have. The notification emails would sometimes prompt me too.
Q4: Yeah, after a few weeks it became automatic to check the LMS in the morning with my coffee. I set it as my browser homepage for a while, which helped. Watching the lecture videos became almost like a ritual—I'd settle in with my notes and visual aids ready. Once I built that habit, it felt weird if I skipped a day.
Q5: The video content was absolutely essential for me—I watched over two hours worth regularly because seeing concepts visually makes them stick. Diagrams, charts, and any visual representations in the materials were gold. I also found the course structure helpful for knowing what was coming next. When resources were organized clearly, I could plan my study sessions much more effectively.
Q6: I'll be honest, the discussion forums didn't draw me in much—I only posted a couple of times. I felt like I could learn the material without that interaction, and sometimes the discussions felt disconnected from what I was actually struggling with. Some of the text-heavy readings I'd skim rather than deeply engage with because they didn't match my learning style. I knew it wasn't ideal, but I had to prioritize.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see how it connected to the bigger picture or the exam. Visual content that helped me understand complex concepts was always worth it. If something felt like busywork or didn't clearly contribute to my understanding, I'd deprioritize it. I also valued activities that gave me a sense of progress—like completing a module felt satisfying.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't always afford. When things got busy, required work took priority, and optional stuff fell away. But sometimes the optional materials were actually more interesting or helpful than the required ones, which was frustrating. I tried to do them when I had breathing room, but that wasn't often enough.
Q9: Absolutely—I downloaded a lot of materials and studied them offline. I'd print diagrams and annotate them by hand, create my own visual summaries on paper. I also used external YouTube videos to supplement concepts that weren't clicking. My physical notebook became just as important as the LMS, maybe more so for actual retention.
Q10: There were many times I'd be reviewing printed materials or my handwritten notes for hours, and none of that showed up anywhere. I'd also work through problems on paper, testing myself without any digital trace. Sometimes I'd discuss concepts with a friend over coffee—real learning happening, zero LMS evidence. My best study sessions often happened away from the screen.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a video or PDF, watch or read it once online, then revisit it multiple times offline. The LMS would only show that initial access, but I might have spent three more hours with that material. It was more convenient to have things saved locally, especially when internet was spotty or I wanted to study somewhere quiet.
Q12: There were a few weeks when personal stuff got heavy and my attendance dropped—that 70% reflects some real struggles. I remember feeling surprised by how quickly things could pile up when I missed a couple of sessions. The mindfulness intervention actually helped me manage those moments better. I'd bounce back, but those dips were real.
Q13: Fatigue was probably my biggest enemy. With six online courses, there were times I'd log into the LMS but my brain was just fried. Competing responsibilities—work, personal life—definitely affected how present I could be. Sometimes I'd have the materials open but couldn't absorb anything meaningful. I learned to recognize when I needed to step back and rest.
Q14: Yes, more than I'd like to admit. I'd have videos playing while my mind wandered somewhere else entirely. Sometimes I'd scroll through materials just to feel like I was doing something, but nothing was sinking in. Those sessions felt wasteful, but I was too tired to do better. The task completion rate probably reflects some of that hollow engagement.
Q15: I definitely postponed some assignments, which is probably why my completion rate wasn't great. Sometimes I avoided things that felt overwhelming or unclear—if I didn't understand what was expected, I'd put it off. I knew it was important, but the anxiety of not knowing where to start was paralyzing. Eventually I'd push through, but not always on time.
Q16: Deadlines were my main driver, honestly. I'd ramp up my LMS activity significantly as due dates approached. Without deadlines, I might have drifted more than I'd like to admit. They created urgency that helped me focus, even if it meant some last-minute stress. My login frequency probably spiked noticeably around assignment periods.
Q17: When I got feedback, it definitely influenced what I focused on next. Positive feedback motivated me to keep going; critical feedback made me revisit materials more carefully. But sometimes feedback came too late to really help with the next assignment. I appreciated when instructors were specific—vague comments didn't give me much to work with.
Q18: The course structure helped me pace myself, even when I fell behind. Having clear modules and deadlines gave me a roadmap. But with six courses running simultaneously, sometimes the structures clashed and I had to make tough choices. A well-organized course was easier to navigate and kept me more engaged overall.
Q19: Visual materials and clear organization made engagement much easier for me. When things were text-heavy or the navigation was confusing, I'd lose motivation. I appreciated when important information was highlighted or presented graphically. The courses that understood different learning styles kept me coming back more consistently.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is trying hard but struggling to produce quality work. They might be reviewing materials repeatedly, trying to understand, but can't translate that into completed assignments. Or maybe they're dealing with perfectionism—engaging a lot but never feeling ready to submit. High activity doesn't always mean productive activity.
Q21: That resonates with me somewhat. I performed reasonably well despite not having the highest engagement metrics because a lot of my real learning happened offline. Someone could watch a video once, take great notes, and master the material without logging in repeatedly. The LMS doesn't capture efficient studying or strong prior knowledge.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you're active, what you click on, how long you're logged in. But they miss the quality of that time entirely. They can't tell if you're deeply focused or just scrolling mindlessly. They definitely miss offline studying, peer discussions, and the emotional experience of learning. It's like measuring exercise by counting gym check-ins.
Q23: Being engaged means I'm genuinely curious and invested in understanding the material, not just going through motions. It's when I'm making connections, asking questions in my head, and wanting to learn more. Real engagement feels active—I'm processing, not just consuming. It's also about caring whether I actually master something, not just pass.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I grew in resilience this semester, or how the mindfulness intervention genuinely helped me cope with stress. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent with printed diagrams spread across my floor, making visual connections. It also wouldn't show that despite some struggles, I surprised myself by pushing through and passing. My journey was more than clicks and timestamps.
